record.field: AdventureLog is a self-hostable travel tracker and trip planner that turns your journeys into an interactive world map and a private travel journal.

AdventureLog is a self-hostable travel tracker and trip planner that turns your journeys into an interactive world map and a private travel journal.

It lets you log the places you have visited and the ones you plan to explore, enriching each location with dates, descriptions, ratings, photos, activities, and trails. Built on a SvelteKit frontend and a Django/PostGIS backend, it pairs detailed geographic data with collaborative itinerary planning, making it a complete companion for documenting past trips and organizing future ones. It targets travelers and households who want full ownership of their travel data instead of relying on closed commercial apps.

Key features
  • Interactive world map of visited and planned locations, with filtering and click-to-add
  • World travel book tracking countries and regions you have explored
  • Trip planning with multi-day itineraries, checklists, notes, flight info, and budgeting
  • Activities and trails (GPX) with detailed maps and stats, including Strava and Wanderer imports
  • Photos, ratings, and rich metadata attached to each location
  • Sharing and real-time collaboration on trips via public links or with other users
Best for

Travelers, families, and self-hosting enthusiasts who want a private, open-source alternative to commercial travel apps to log their adventures, visualize them on a map, and plan future trips collaboratively.

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR. # Si POSTGRES_USER/POSTGRES_DB vienen vacíos (la imagen usa 'postgres' por defecto), # caemos a 'postgres': con $POSTGRES_USER vacío pg_dump intentaría el rol "root" del SO # y fallaría con «role "root" does not exist». docker compose exec -T db sh -c 'pg_dump -U "${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}" -d "${POSTGRES_DB:-${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}}" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura (mismo default a 'postgres') docker compose up -d db for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T db sh -c 'pg_isready -U "${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T db sh -c 'pg_restore -U "${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}" -d "${POSTGRES_DB:-${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}}" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T db sh -c "psql -U \${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres} -d \${POSTGRES_DB:-${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}} -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
